rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of validation effort spent on low-risk waste-handling elements disproportionate to their assessed risk
rows.imp_rat: Over-validating low-risk waste elements wastes qualification resources but does not create compliance risk, so it matters mainly for effort efficiency.
rows.sat_rat: Risk-based approaches theoretically trim low-risk effort, but conservative defaults often keep the status quo over-testing waste items.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree to which an inconsistent qualification verdict erodes peers' trust in my validation authority
rows.imp_rat: An inconsistent qualification verdict erodes peers' trust, mattering to standing but less existential than an outright field failure.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized decision criteria promote consistency, yet subjective waste-risk calls still produce occasional verdict variability.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of reworking the financial case when finance challenges the hazardous-waste cost baseline
rows.imp_rat: Reworking the financial case when finance challenges the hazardous-waste baseline drains the buyer's time and delays approval.
rows.sat_rat: Buyers can pre-align with finance, but weak cost baselines for hazardous streams still provoke challenges and rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time to obtain funding approval from financial authorities for the waste-system acquisition
rows.imp_rat: Slow funding approval stalls the acquisition and can push schedule risk onto the buyer, though it is a recurring rather than existential concern.
rows.sat_rat: Established capital-approval workflows move at a predictable pace, but hazardous-waste spend often triggers extra scrutiny that lengthens cycles.

rows.source_job: feel confident the supplier will deliver
rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of uncertainty about the supplier's track record on prior hazardous-waste system commitments
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about a supplier's prior hazardous-waste track record undermines the buyer's confidence in selection and defensibility.
rows.sat_rat: Reference checks and past-performance data are obtainable but often incomplete for specialized hazardous-waste commitments.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of being seen by leadership as having overpaid for the hazardous-waste solution
rows.imp_rat: Being seen by leadership as having overpaid strikes at the buyer's identity as a value steward, a meaningful status stake.
rows.sat_rat: Competitive bidding provides some price defensibility, but sparse benchmarks for niche waste systems leave overpayment perceptions possible.

rows.stmt: Reduce the likelihood of losing standing as a disciplined steward when a selected supplier fails to deliver
rows.imp_rat: Losing standing when a chosen supplier fails threatens the buyer's reputation as a disciplined sourcing steward.
rows.sat_rat: Due-diligence processes offer partial cover, but supplier failures still reflect on the buyer despite good process.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of revision cycles required to bring the business case to approval-ready quality
rows.imp_rat: Excessive revision cycles delay approval and consume the director's and staff's time, though the quality of the decision matters more than the churn.
rows.sat_rat: Business-case templates help, but multiple revision rounds to reach approval quality remain common in current capital processes.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of the funded waste containment capacity that sits unused across the facility's hazardous waste streams
rows.imp_rat: Idle funded containment capacity is wasted capital the director must answer for, though it is a secondary concern versus compliance and budget integrity.
rows.sat_rat: Sizing is usually done to peak/worst-case streams, so some over-provisioning and unused capacity is tolerated as normal in current practice.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to compare this investment's merits against the alternative capital requests in the same funding cycle
rows.imp_rat: Faster head-to-head comparison against competing requests helps the director run an efficient funding cycle, but is a convenience relative to decision correctness.
rows.sat_rat: Comparing a compliance project against revenue projects lacks common metrics, so comparison remains laborious in the status quo.

rows.stmt: Minimize the number of waste stream movements that go unrecorded between generation and accumulation
rows.imp_rat: Unrecorded movements break the chain-of-custody the technician must maintain, but this is more a compliance concern than an immediate operational blocker to the physical job.
rows.sat_rat: Paper logs or manifests capture most movements in GMP facilities, but manual recording during busy runs leaves gaps that current practice tolerates.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time spent locating waste containers at scattered points of generation across the suite during a collection run
rows.imp_rat: Time hunting for scattered containers is pure productivity drag on the collection run, moderately important but not a safety-critical failure.
rows.sat_rat: Designated hold points help, but without location tracking the technician still spends real time searching across a large suite today.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time waste sits idle when a blocked corridor or door prevents it from reaching its designated point
rows.imp_rat: Blocked corridors leaving waste idle stall the transport job and can back up suite capacity, a moderate operational disruption for the technician.
rows.sat_rat: Alternate routes and airlock scheduling exist, but the technician often has no quick way to resolve a blockage and simply waits, so idle time persists.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time spent negotiating competing stakeholder priorities into a workable disposal design
rows.imp_rat: Time spent negotiating priorities is a productivity drain but secondary to whether the resulting design is safe and compliant.
rows.sat_rat: No structured tool accelerates stakeholder negotiation today, so it remains a lengthy, ad hoc effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved qualification barriers blocking eligible suppliers from the sourcing process
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved qualification barriers shrink the eligible supplier pool, reducing competitive leverage the buyer relies on.
rows.sat_rat: Pharma qualification requirements are stringent and slow to clear, so eligible-supplier barriers routinely persist.

rows.source_job: feel reassured the funding was justified
rows.stmt: Reduce the likelihood of feeling exposed when the hazardous-waste investment's payback is questioned
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed when payback is questioned is a personal-stakes emotion tied to the buyer's accountability for the investment.
rows.sat_rat: Payback claims for hazardous-waste systems rest on uncertain savings, so the buyer remains vulnerable when challenged.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time spent returning to the suite to relabel a container after a labeling discrepancy is found
rows.imp_rat: Return trips to relabel a container waste the technician's time and disrupt the run, a moderate efficiency loss rather than a core hazard.
rows.sat_rat: Nothing in the status quo lets a technician correct a labeling discrepancy in place, so relabel round-trips remain a recurring nuisance.

rows.stmt: Minimize the frequency of second-guessing a routing decision made under time pressure during a run
rows.imp_rat: Second-guessing routing under time pressure adds mental load and slows runs, a real but secondary emotional strain relative to physical hazards.
rows.sat_rat: Experience and signage give confidence for routine streams, but ambiguous cases still leave the technician uncertain during pressured runs today.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of flow congestion at routing chokepoints when designing the waste routing through the fill-finish layout
rows.imp_rat: Flow congestion at chokepoints affects operational efficiency but is a lower-stakes concern than safety or sterility for this engineer.
rows.sat_rat: Layout and material-flow tools address congestion adequately in most facility designs, though tight cleanroom footprints still bottleneck.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of specification revisions after suppliers flag ambiguous hazardous-waste handling clauses
rows.imp_rat: Spec revisions after suppliers flag ambiguous clauses add cycle time, though they are recoverable within the sourcing process.
rows.sat_rat: Iterative RFP clarification rounds catch ambiguities but hazardous-handling clauses commonly require several revisions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of resubmitting the funding request after approver objections
rows.imp_rat: Repeated funding resubmissions cost the buyer time and credibility but are a procedural annoyance rather than a core stakes item.
rows.sat_rat: Structured business-case templates reduce resubmission somewhat, though approver objections on hazardous spend still trigger rework.

rows.source_job: feel confident the supplier will deliver
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt about the selected supplier's ability to deliver reliably across the contract term
rows.imp_rat: Doubt about the supplier's delivery reliability weighs on the buyer emotionally, though it maps onto the functional supplier-risk work.
rows.sat_rat: References and contracts provide some reassurance, but the thin qualified-supplier pool sustains residual doubt.

rows.source_job: feel reassured the funding was justified
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of anxiety that the approved spend cannot be defended the moment finance reviews it
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety that the spend cannot be defended in finance review is a real emotional load but secondary to the functional case itself.
rows.sat_rat: A solid documented business case eases this anxiety, but weak hazardous-waste value data leaves lingering unease.

rows.stmt: Reduce the likelihood that the funded asset cannot be redeployed to other facility waste streams as production demand shifts
rows.imp_rat: Redeployability across shifting waste streams protects the asset's value, but for a compliance-oriented director it is a secondary flexibility concern.
rows.sat_rat: Modular containment options exist, but most systems are specified to current streams with limited redeployment flexibility today.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of non-hazardous waste mistakenly routed into higher-cost hazardous disposal streams
rows.imp_rat: Routing non-hazardous waste into costly hazardous streams is mainly a cost issue, secondary to the manager's safety-first mandate.
rows.sat_rat: Over-routing to hazardous is a safe default that current practice tolerates, so it is 'handled' safely but not cost-optimally.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of variability in recurring disposal cost across reporting periods
rows.imp_rat: Disposal cost predictability matters to the site but sits outside the EHS Manager's core safety-and-compliance job, being more a finance/operations concern.
rows.sat_rat: Disposal contracts and manifests give reasonable cost visibility, though volumes swing period to period with production and conservative segregation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of disposal volume attributable to over-conservative segregation of mixed waste streams
rows.imp_rat: Trimming over-conservative segregation volume is a cost-efficiency aim that competes with the manager's safety-first instinct to over-segregate.
rows.sat_rat: Conservative segregation is deliberately accepted today for safety, so the outcome is under-served but by design rather than by failure.
